lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: Is execution done on Cawdor? Are not

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: Those in commission yet return’d?

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: My liege,

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: They are not yet come back. But I have spoke

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: With one that saw him die: who did report

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: That very frankly he confess’d his treasons,

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Implored your highness’ pardon and set forth

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: A deep repentance: nothing in his life

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: Became him like the leaving it; he died

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: As one that had been studied in his death

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: To throw away the dearest thing he owed,

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: As ’twere a careless trifle.

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: There’s no art

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: To find the mind’s construction in the face:

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: He was a gentleman on whom I built

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: An absolute trust.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The service and the loyalty I owe,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: In doing it, pays itself. Your highness’ part

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Is to receive our duties; and our duties

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Are to your throne and state children and servants,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Which do but what they should, by doing every thing

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Safe toward your love and honour.

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: Welcome hither:

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: I have begun to plant thee, and will labour

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: To make thee full of growing. Noble Banquo,

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: That hast no less deserved, nor must be known

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: No less to have done so, let me enfold thee

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: And hold thee to my heart.

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: There if I grow,

lines.speaker: BANQUO
lines.text: The harvest is your own.

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: My plenteous joys,

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: Wanton in fulness, seek to hide themselves

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: In drops of sorrow. Sons, kinsmen, thanes,

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: And you whose places are the nearest, know

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: We will establish our estate upon

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: Our eldest, Malcolm, whom we name hereafter

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: The Prince of Cumberland; which honour must

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: Not unaccompanied invest him only,

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: But signs of nobleness, like stars, shall shine

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: On all deservers. From hence to Inverness,

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: And bind us further to you.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The rest is labour, which is not used for you:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I’ll be myself the harbinger and make joyful

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The hearing of my wife with your approach;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: So humbly take my leave.

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: My worthy Cawdor!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The Prince of Cumberland! that is a step

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: On which I must fall down, or else o’erleap,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: For in my way it lies. Stars, hide your fires;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Let not light see my black and deep desires:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The eye wink at the hand; yet let that be,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Which the eye fears, when it is done, to see.

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: True, worthy Banquo; he is full so valiant,

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: And in his commendations I am fed;

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: It is a banquet to me. Let’s after him,

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: Whose care is gone before to bid us welcome:

lines.speaker: DUNCAN
lines.text: It is a peerless kinsman.

stage_directions.text: Flourish. Enter Duncan, Malcolm, Donalbain, Lennox, and Attendants.
Enter Macbeth, Banquo, Ross, and Angus.

O worthiest cousin! The sin of my ingratitude even now Was heavy on me: thou art so far before That swiftest wing of recompense is slow To overtake thee. Would thou hadst less deserved, That the proportion both of thanks and payment Might have been mine! only I have left to say, More is thy due than more than all can pay.

glosses.definition: rehearsed his execution like a lesson
complete confidence — which he now places in Macbeth
the formal title for the heir to the Scottish throne — a roadblock Macbeth now sees
a town in the Scottish Highlands; Macbeth's castle
Macbeth's first dark prayer — asking the heavens to conceal his intentions
murderous ambitions he is beginning to admit to himself
close (my eye) to what the hand does — separation of moral conscience from murderous action

annotations.title: 'No art to find the mind's construction in the face'
annotations.body:
Duncan's line about the first Thane of Cawdor — that there is no art to read inner mind from outer face — is delivered at the exact moment Macbeth, his new Thane of Cawdor, enters the stage. The dramatic irony is too pointed to miss: Duncan's epistemological humility is exactly right, and exactly fatal. He cannot read the face of the man he is about to host.

This is the play's first serious engagement with its central moral problem. Hamlet asked whether the ghost's report could be trusted. *Othello* asks whether Desdemona's face tells the truth. *Macbeth* asks whether any face, king or subject, is legible at all. Duncan says no, and proceeds as if yes.

annotations.title: 'Stars, hide your fires'
annotations.body:
Macbeth's aside concludes with his first direct prayer for darkness. 'Stars, hide your fires; / Let not light see my black and deep desires.' The dissociation is already advanced: the hand must not know what the eye sees. This will become the play's recurring grammar — hands estranged from their owners, eyes covered, night invoked against day.

The Cleanth Brooks reading of the play's imagery picks up here. Darkness is not metaphor in *Macbeth*; it is invocation. Lady Macbeth in 1.5 will speak the complement of this prayer — the 'blanket of the dark' — and the play's central murders will all happen under self-summoned night.
sources: Cleanth Brooks, 'The Naked Babe and the Cloak of Manliness' (1947)

trials.prompt: Duncan announces Malcolm as 'Prince of Cumberland' (line 39). What structural function does this serve?
options: It names a political obstacle Macbeth sees immediately — the visible block on his way to the crown

trials.prompt: What is the dramatic irony of Duncan saying 'There's no art / To find the mind's construction in the face'?
options: Duncan says it just as the new Cawdor (Macbeth) enters — he cannot read the face of the man about to kill him

trials.prompt: Macbeth prays 'Stars, hide your fires.' This begins:
options: A pattern in which the play's central murders are all invoked under self-summoned darkness
